worth_it_explanation: Creative blocking decides which advertisers, categories, and domains may run on a publisher's inventory, so it sits at the point where brand standards meet revenue. A block list that has drifted away from the publisher's standards can let through advertisers that conflict with the brand, and a block list that has grown too broad can suppress demand that the publisher meant to keep. The check is a review of the block lists already set in Microsoft Monetize, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete blocking decision the publisher can test against revenue and brand fit.
intro: Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize) lets a publisher block advertisers, content categories, and advertiser domains so that demand which conflicts with the publisher's standards does not clear on its inventory. This subcategory is about the block lists themselves, whether they match the publisher's brand standards and whether they are scoped so they protect the brand without suppressing more revenue than intended. These checks confirm that the blocking controls reflect a deliberate standard rather than settings left untouched, and that the blocks are no broader than the standard requires.

audit_checks.title: Advertiser, category, and domain block lists are not maintained against the publisher's standards
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Block lists that drift away from the publisher's brand standards let through advertisers, categories, or domains the publisher would refuse if asked directly. The result is creative running next to editorial content that the publisher did not intend to host, which is a brand-fit problem that surfaces when an advertiser or a reader notices the mismatch rather than at the moment the block list went stale.

educate.base:
Microsoft Monetize gives a publisher several blocking levers that work together. Advertiser-level blocks stop a named buyer from clearing on the inventory. Category blocks use the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy to stop whole content categories, e.g., gambling or alcohol, from serving. Domain blocks (the blocked advertiser domains list, carried as 'badv' in the OpenRTB request the platform sends to demand) stop creative whose landing-page domain matches an entry. Together these decide which demand a publisher is willing to host.

The blocking controls work as written, so the audit question is not whether they function but whether they still match the publisher's standards. A block list is a snapshot of a decision made at one point in time. Brand standards change, a publisher signs a direct deal that should override an old block, a category the publisher once allowed becomes one it now refuses, and a new advertiser appears that conflicts with the brand. None of those changes update the block list on their own. When the list and the standard drift apart, demand that the publisher would refuse if asked directly clears anyway because the block that would have caught it was never added.

Microsoft Monetize is built on the OpenRTB protocol and is Prebid-compatible, so the blocking attributes it sends to demand follow the OpenRTB blocking model: blocked advertiser domains, blocked advertiser categories, and blocked creative attributes. Reviewing the block lists against the publisher's current standards is a matter of reading what is set, comparing it to what the publisher says they want to refuse, and recording where the two diverge.

A few signs point to block lists that have not been maintained:
  • The block lists carry no record of when they were last reviewed against the publisher's brand standards
  • Category blocks do not match the categories the publisher names when asked what content they refuse to run ads against
  • An advertiser the publisher names as off-brand is not on the advertiser block list
  • Domain blocks contain entries no one can explain, or omit domains the publisher has since decided to refuse
You may want to confirm that the block lists reflect the publisher's current standards rather than a setting left untouched. One approach is to ask the publisher to name the advertisers, categories, and domains they refuse to host, then compare that list against what Microsoft Monetize actually blocks.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, brand standards often turn on the editorial subject matter, e.g., a news outlet that refuses gambling creative next to coverage of addiction, or a family-focused publisher that blocks alcohol categories. You may want to walk the publisher's category block list against the IAB content categories that conflict with their editorial mission specifically, which are the blocks most likely to matter to readers and to the direct-sold advertisers who value the editorial context.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the standard often turns on competitive conflict, e.g., a retailer that refuses advertiser domains belonging to direct competitors running next to its own product content. You may want to confirm the advertiser and domain block lists carry the competitors the publisher wants to keep off its inventory. A competitor's creative on a product page is a brand-fit problem the publisher would catch immediately if asked.

learn_more.source_title: ORTB2 Blocking Module for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Microsoft Monetize's detailed configuration documentation for advertiser, category, and domain blocking is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the advertiser, category, and domain block lists in Microsoft Monetize against the standards the publisher names, and look for any record of when each list was last reviewed.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the brand-safety or blocking controls and list the active advertiser blocks, category blocks, and blocked advertiser domains.
For the category blocks, note which IAB content categories are set and whether they map to the content the publisher says they refuse to run ads against.
Ask the publisher to name the advertisers, content categories, and competitor domains they refuse to host, and record each one.
Compare the publisher's stated standards against what is actually blocked, and record every advertiser, category, or domain the publisher names that is not on a block list.
Ask when each block list was last reviewed and record the date, or record that no review date is known.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the advertiser, category, and domain blocks set in Microsoft Monetize alongside the standards the publisher names. Block lists that match the publisher's stated standards, with a recent review date on record, pass. A block list that omits an advertiser, category, or domain the publisher names as off-brand, or that carries no record of when it was last reviewed, is a fail recorded with each gap between the list and the standard.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the advertiser, category, and domain blocks and the standards the publisher names above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the advertiser, category, and domain block lists in Microsoft Monetize for {{site_name}} do not match the brand standards the publisher names, so demand the publisher would refuse can clear on the inventory anyway. A block list reflects a decision made at one point in time, and when standards change without the list changing with them, off-brand creative runs because the block that would have caught it was never added. {{voice_recommend}} asking the publisher to name the advertisers, content categories, and domains they refuse to host, comparing that against what Microsoft Monetize actually blocks, and adding the missing entries. After the change, set a date to review the block lists against the standards so the two stay aligned.

audit_checks.title: Block lists are over-broad and suppress more demand than the standard requires
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block list that reaches wider than the publisher's standard removes demand that would have been acceptable, which lowers competition in the auction and the clearing price along with it. The lost revenue does not appear as an error, it appears as fill and price that are quietly lower than they could be, so an over-broad block costs money without ever flagging itself.

educate.base:
Blocking protects the brand, and every block also removes demand. Each advertiser, category, or domain a publisher blocks is one fewer source of competition in the auction, so a block that is wider than the publisher's standard requires suppresses revenue that the standard never meant to give up. The cost shows up not as an error but as lower fill and a lower clearing price because the demand that would have raised the price was filtered out before the auction ran.

Over-broad blocking takes a few common shapes. A category block set at a high tier of the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy can sweep in subcategories the publisher would have accepted, e.g., blocking an entire parent category to refuse one narrow topic within it. A blocked advertiser domains list can carry stale entries for advertisers the publisher no longer objects to, or duplicates and typos that block nothing useful while adding noise. An advertiser block added during one campaign dispute can outlive the dispute and keep suppressing that buyer long after the reason has passed.

Because Microsoft Monetize is built on the OpenRTB protocol, its blocks travel to demand as blocked advertiser domains and blocked categories on every request, so a block applied broadly applies to all the demand that request reaches. The audit question is whether each block earns its place, that is, whether it maps to a standard the publisher would state plainly, or whether it is wider than the standard and is suppressing demand for no current reason.

A few signs point to blocks that are broader than the standard requires:
  • Category blocks are set at a high tier of the taxonomy when the publisher's objection is to a narrow subcategory within it
  • The blocked advertiser domains list contains stale entries, duplicates, or typos that no one can tie to a current standard
  • An advertiser block traces back to a one-time dispute that has since been resolved
  • Fill rate or clearing price on inventory under heavy blocking sits below comparable inventory with lighter blocking, with no brand reason for the gap
You may want to confirm that each block maps to a standard the publisher still holds rather than reaching wider than the standard. One approach is to walk the block lists entry by entry, keep the blocks the publisher can tie to a current standard, and remove the ones that no longer have a reason.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a broad category block can suppress demand the editorial brand would have accepted, e.g., blocking a whole parent category to refuse one sensitive topic within it. You may want to narrow category blocks to the specific subcategories that conflict with the editorial standard, so the brand stays protected while the acceptable demand within the parent category keeps competing.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, an advertiser or domain block added to keep one competitor off the inventory can be wider than intended if it sweeps in adjacent advertisers that pose no conflict. You may want to confirm each blocked domain maps to a competitor the publisher actually wants to refuse, so the block protects against the conflict without removing unrelated retail demand that would have competed for the product-page inventory.

learn_more.source_title: ORTB2 Blocking Module for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Microsoft Monetize's detailed configuration documentation for advertiser, category, and domain blocking is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each entry on the advertiser, category, and domain block lists for a standard the publisher still holds, and read fill rate and clearing price on heavily blocked inventory against comparable inventory.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the blocking controls and list every advertiser block, category block, and blocked advertiser domain.
For each entry, ask the publisher whether it maps to a standard they still hold, and record the reason or record that none is known.
For category blocks, note the taxonomy tier each block is set at and whether the publisher's objection is to the whole category or to a narrower subcategory within it.
Flag stale entries, duplicates, and typos on the domain list that cannot be tied to a current standard.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by ad unit or placement with fill rate and average clearing price.
Compare inventory under heavy blocking against comparable inventory with lighter blocking to see whether fill rate or clearing price sits lower.
Note any case where heavily blocked inventory clears lower with no brand reason for the gap, which points to blocking that is suppressing acceptable demand.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block alongside the standard the publisher ties it to and, where available, the fill rate and clearing price on the inventory it affects. Block lists where every entry maps to a current standard pass. A block list carrying entries the publisher cannot tie to a standard, category blocks set wider than the objection requires, or heavily blocked inventory clearing below comparable inventory with no brand reason is a fail recorded with each block that suppresses demand without a current standard behind it.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block and the standard the publisher ties it to above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the block lists in Microsoft Monetize for {{site_name}} reach wider than the publisher's brand standard requires, so demand that would have been acceptable is filtered out before the auction runs. Each block removes a source of competition, so an over-broad block lowers fill and clearing price without flagging itself as an error. {{voice_recommend}} walking the advertiser, category, and domain blocks entry by entry, keeping the blocks the publisher can tie to a current standard, narrowing category blocks to the subcategories the objection actually covers, and removing stale or unexplained entries. After the change, recheck fill rate and clearing price on the affected inventory to confirm the narrower blocking recovered the acceptable demand.
